Deaths registered weekly in Scotland








FAQs





1. What is the difference between registered, occurred and expected deaths?
Registered deaths are counted based on the date the death is officially recorded with the General Register Office.
Occurred deaths (death occurrences) are based on the actual date the person died, regardless of when the death was registered.
Expected deaths are an estimate of how many deaths would normally occur, based on historical trends. These are used to compare with current figures.
2. Why might deaths appear in a different week than when they occurred?
Deaths are shown based on the registration date, not the date of death. This means some deaths may appear in a later week than when they actually happened. Most deaths in Northern Ireland are registered within 5 days Delays can occur, especially around public holidays
3. Are weekly figures final?
No — weekly figures are provisional. They may change as more data becomes available. Data are reviewed and updated quarterly as part of quality assurance
4. How are expected deaths calculated?
Expected deaths are calculated using statistical methods based on historical data trends. More information on the current method of estimating expected deaths and calculating excess deaths is available in the ONS Methodology Paper at: Estimating excess deaths in the UK, methodology changes - Office for National Statistics (ons.gov.uk)
5. What counts as a COVID‑19 or flu-related death?
COVID‑19 deaths include any mention of coronavirus (confirmed or suspected) anywhere on the death certificate Flu or pneumonia deaths include any mention of these conditions on the death certificate
6. How are deaths assigned to areas (LGDs)?
Deaths are assigned based on the person’s usual residence (which can include care homes). If the person did not usually live in Northern Ireland, the death is assigned to the place of death
7. What do the place of death categories mean?
Care/Nursing homes: Only includes deaths that occurred in those settings Other: Includes deaths at non-usual residences or any other locations not listed

title: About
text:
This page provides background information about the weekly death registrations dashboard.
The dashboard gives a weekly analysis of deaths registered in Northern Ireland.

title: About
text:
Background information
Registration based data presented in this spreadsheet are provisional.
This dashboard is refreshed weekly, at 9:30am each Friday when new data is released.
The dashboard was last updated on 08 June 2026.
Information on the quality of deaths data is available from the Background Quality Report and the Quality Assurance of Administrative Data (QAAD) document for deaths at the links below:

BQR Deaths
QAAD Deaths

title: About
text:
Data source(s)
The data for the dashboard is sourced and can be downloaded from the NISRA Data Portal. ** NEED TO UPDATE LINK WHEN LIVE ON DP **
The tables giving a weekly analysis of deaths registered in Northern Ireland can also be found published on the NISRA website.

title: About
text:
Things you need to know
Deaths occurring in Northern Ireland are registered on the NI General Register Office’s Registration System (NIROS). Daily extracts of registration records from NIROS are processed by the NISRA Vital Statistics Unit.
Cause of death coding to the ICD-10 classification is carried out by ONS on NISRA’s behalf. This means that our quarterly statistics are the first output to report deaths according to underlying cause. These statistics are currently available up to Quarter 4 2025.
Due to user demand, NISRA will continue to publish a weekly, provisional estimate of Covid-19 related deaths in Northern Ireland, but will not include any other breakdowns. Further information on deaths caused by Covid-19 will be available in the Registrar General Quarterly Tables.

Weekly deaths - ending 29 May 2026
To meet user needs, NISRA publish timely but provisional counts of death registrations in Northern Ireland in our Weekly Deaths provisional dataset. To allow time for registration and processing, these figures are published 7 days after the week ends.


Provisional data and revisions
These provisional tables are updated and published on a weekly basis to meet immediate user need and interest. More detailed data, including cause of death, are available in the Registrar General Quarterly tables, which includes data that has gone through more rigurous validations, but are still provisional. Following the release of quarterly tables the weekly totals are revised to reflect these further validations so there may be some minimal change. Data are finalised through the publication of the Annual Report of the Registrar General, after which final weekly death registrations for that year will be added to the historical weekly deaths data file available at https://www.nisra.gov.uk/publications/historical-weekly-deaths-data. Final data may differ from provisional data for a number of reasons. Final data has undergone an extensive set of quality assurance checks to ensure that all registration information is as correct and complete as possible. Final data also reflect cases where deaths have been re-registered at a later date in the same year by the informant or next of kin in order to change some detail within the registration, for example name spelling or date of birth. Until data for the calendar year have been finalised we will take the most recent registration as the more accurate. As data are based on registration date, it means that the week or even month to which re-registered cases relate to can change. This only happens in a very small number of cases (less than 0.1% of cases over the year) each year so while it does not change the overall number of deaths registered in a year, the individual weekly or even monthly totals and breakdowns by various characteristics may change by a small amount.
Weekly deaths data last revised to reflect latest quarterly validations: 03.04.2026
Weekly expected deaths data last revised to reflect latest population estimates/projections: 03.10.2025


Data over public holidays
The number of deaths registered can be impacted by General Registration Office closures around statutory holidays. Care should therefore be taken in interpreting weekly data for the week of, and potentially the week after, any public or bank holiday, especially if making comparisons with other weeks or years. For reference, such holidays in 2026 are listed in the table below. Please note, this list is not exhaustive as GRO office closures may also include additional days around statutory holidays, and opening hours/days can vary across Council areas. Upcoming holidays are also listed on Gov.uk - Northern Ireland Public Holidays.


This is an Accredited Official Statistics publication
Accredited official statistics are called National Statistics in the Statistics and Registration Service Act 2007.
Accredited Official Statistics are produced to high professional standards set out in the Code of Practice for Official Statistics. They are produced free from any political interference.
These accredited official statistics were independently reviewed by the Office for Statistics Regulation in April 2012. A new Accredited Official Statistics badge has been produced in line with the change from National Statistics to Accredited Official Statistics. While the badge has changed, the meaning behind it has remained the same.
They comply with the standards of trustworthiness, quality and value in the Code of Practice for Statistics and should be labelled ‘accredited official statistics’.
Our statistical practice is regulated by the Office for Statistics Regulation (OSR). OSR sets the standards of trustworthiness, quality and value in the Code of Practice for Statistics that all producers of official statistics should adhere to.
View the full assessement of Demography and Vital Events Statistics for Northern Ireland.
You are welcome to contact us directly with any comments about how we meet these standards by emailing demography@nisra.gov.uk. Alternatively, you can contact OSR by emailing regulation@statistics.gov.uk or via the OSR website.
The new method for estimating expected deaths and therefore excess deaths are Official Statistics in Development.
Estimates produced by the new excess deaths methodology will be regularly reviewed, with further refinements to the approach being undertaken if necessary. As such, estimates of excess deaths produced by the new methodology will be labelled as Official Statistics in Development while further review, testing and development work is undertaken. Further information on Official Statistics in Development is available at: OSR - Official Statistics in Development
